coredata.dc:title: Enhanced bioaccumulation efficiency and tolerance for Cd (Ⅱ) in Arabidopsis thaliana by amphoteric nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qiong
Liu, Beibei
Man, Han
Chen, Long
Wang, Xiuli
Tu, Jiangping
Guo, Zhijiang
Jin, Gong
Lou, Jun
Ci, Lijie
coredata.dc:description: Amphoteric nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) that prepared environmentally friendly have rich functional groups, such as carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, carbonyl, etc. Through electrostatic attraction and complexation between the chemical groups and metal ions, N-CDs present excellent adsorption capacity for Cd2+ in heavy polluted water with the saturated adsorption weight of 559 mg g−1. The investigation of interaction between N-CDs, Cd2+ and Arabidopsis thaliana reveals that N-CDs (from 4 mg kg−1 to 8 mg kg−1) can dramatically enhance Cd bioaccumulation of plants by 58.3% of unit biomass and 260% of individual seedling when the plants were cultivated for 10 days under Cd stress (from 10 mg kg−1 to 50 mg kg−1). Simultaneously, N-CDs significantly alleviate the toxicity caused by high Cd stress on Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings growth. N-CDs induce higher germination rate (maximum: 2.5-fold), higher biomass (maximum: 3.7-fold), better root development (maximum: 1.4-fold), higher photosynthetic efficiency and higher antioxidant capacity in plants under Cd stress. When the Cd and N-CDs concentration are respective 20 mg kg-1 and 4 mg kg-1, the enzyme activities of the catalase and peroxidase increased to 2.73-fold and 1.45-fold, respectively. This research prove the potential application of amphoteric N-CDs in phytoremediation because N-CDs greatly mitigate the growth retardation of plant caused by Cd2+ even with the extremely increased Cd2+ concentration in vivo.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen doped
Bioaccumulation
Tolerance
Cd stress
Heavy metal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N-CDs alleviate the toxicity of Cd2+ to plants

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Startup Funding of Distinguished Professorship of “ 1000 Talents Program ” ( 31370086963030 ), the high-level Talents' Discipline Construction Fund of Shandong University ( 31370089963078 ), the “ Taishan Scholar Program ” ( 11370085961006 ), Shandong Provincial Science and Technology Major Project ( 2016GGX104001 , 2017CXGC1010 , 2018JMRH0211 , ZR2017MEM002 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University ( 2016JC005 , 2017JC042 and 2017JC010 ) at Shandong University .

This work was supported by Startup Funding of Distinguished Professorship of “1000 Talents Program” (31370086963030), the high-level Talents' Discipline Construction Fund of Shandong University (31370089963078), the “Taishan Scholar Program” (11370085961006), Shandong Provincial Science and Technology Major Project (2016GGX104001,2017CXGC1010, 2018JMRH0211, ZR2017MEM002), and the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (2016JC005, 2017JC042 and 2017JC010) at Shandong University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology characterizations of the N-CDs materials. (a) TEM pictures of the N-CDs. (b) HR- TEM picture of the N-CDs. The specified part in the rectangle of (b) is shown in (c). (d) Raman spectrum of the N-CDs. (e) Fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs solution of 1 mg ml−1 at 25 °C. (f) FT-IR spectrum of the N-CDs. (g–i) XPS spectra of the N-CDs.

Adsorption of Cd
2+
by the N-CDs. (a) Supposed adsorption mechanism of the N-CDs. (b) Adsorption curves of the N-CDs and Cd. Insert is the TEM picture of the N-CDs-Cd complex.

Cd
2+
accumulation in Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings under laboratory conditions. (a) Cd accumulation in unite dry biomass. (b) Cd accumulation by individual plant. (c) Potential mechanism of N-CDs affecting plants on uptaking Cd2+ ions.

Effects of the N-CDs on plant growth and development under different levels of Cd
2+
stress. (a) Effects of N-CDs on the germination rate of the A. thaliana seeds under Cd2+ stress. (b) Phenotype of the A. thaliana on different medium. (c) Length of main root of the A. thaliana seedlings under Cd stress. (d, e) Fresh and dry biomass of the A. thaliana seedlings cultivated for 10 days under Cd2+ stress. (f) Total chlorophyll content in A. thaliana leaves under Cd2+ stress. (g) Polysaccharides content in A. thaliana leaves under Cd2+ stress. Date represent F-values at 0.05 levels. *P < 0.05. **P < 0.01. ***P < 0.001.
Influence of N-CDs on antioxidant system of plant.

head.ce:title: Enhanced bioaccumulation efficiency and tolerance for Cd (Ⅱ) in Arabidopsis thaliana by amphoteric nitrogen-doped carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Xinna International Hi-Tech Material Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Xinna International Hi-Tech Material Co., Ltd, Beijing 100076, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amphoteric nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) that prepared environmentally friendly have rich functional groups, such as carboxyl, amino, hydroxyl, carbonyl, etc. Through electrostatic attraction and complexation between the chemical groups and metal ions, N-CDs present excellent adsorption capacity for Cd2+ in heavy polluted water with the saturated adsorption weight of 559 mg g−1. The investigation of interaction between N-CDs, Cd2+ and Arabidopsis thaliana reveals that N-CDs (from 4 mg kg−1 to 8 mg kg−1) can dramatically enhance Cd bioaccumulation of plants by 58.3% of unit biomass and 260% of individual seedling when the plants were cultivated for 10 days under Cd stress (from 10 mg kg−1 to 50 mg kg−1). Simultaneously, N-CDs significantly alleviate the toxicity caused by high Cd stress on Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings growth. N-CDs induce higher germination rate (maximum: 2.5-fold), higher biomass (maximum: 3.7-fold), better root development (maximum: 1.4-fold), higher photosynthetic efficiency and higher antioxidant capacity in plants under Cd stress. When the Cd and N-CDs concentration are respective 20 mg kg-1 and 4 mg kg-1, the enzyme activities of the catalase and peroxidase increased to 2.73-fold and 1.45-fold, respectively. This research prove the potential application of amphoteric N-CDs in phytoremediation because N-CDs greatly mitigate the growth retardation of plant caused by Cd2+ even with the extremely increased Cd2+ concentration in vivo.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrialization and urbanization progress have introduced heavy metal pollution in extensive land mass and water bodies. In these environments, heavy metals are not biodegradable and can be accumulated for extended period of time (Hu et al., 2009). Furthermore, these pollutants can enrich along the food chain, causing toxicity from field to table (Vikrant et al., 2019; Tsuchiya, 1976). The main source of heavy metal pollutants is the wastewater from modern chemical industries such as metallurgy, fossil fuel, mining, tannery, battery, paper and fertilizers and pesticides (Pyrzynska, 2010; Zuverza-Mena et al., 2017). Cadmium is one of the most notorious heavy metal pollutant due to its accute toxicity to human health even in low concentration, causing severe illness such as Itai-itai disease (Tsuchiya, 1976). Since Cd2+ could reduce photosynthesis by destroying chloroplast, reducing mineral nutrition uptake and inducing oxidative stress by increasing the reactive oxygen species production (ROS) (Aibibu et al., 2010; Gallego et al., 2012; Nagajyoti et al., 2010), cadmium in soil can lead to various phytotoxic symptoms, including chlorosis, growth retardation, and plants death (Abbas et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2016).

Many methods including chemical precipitation, membrane filtration, ion-exchange, liquid extraction, reverse osmosis and adsorption have been tested to remove cadmium from polluted soil and water (Chen et al., 2018a, 2018b; Li et al., 2003; Tofighy and Mohammadi, 2011), but most of the measures are costly and far away from practical applications. Moreover, some methods can even degrade the valuable components of soils and restrain the protophyte growth and crops yields (Song et al., 2019). Currently, phytoremediation is considered as the most effective and affordable solution to remove inactive metal pollutants from contaminated soil and water, using plants to remove, degrade, or immobilize toxic chemicals (Song et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Over the past several years, some cadmium hyperaccumulating plants have been discovered to be effective in phytoremediation of Cd pollution, such as Festuca arundinacea, Cynara cardunculus L. and Brassica juncea L. (Luo et al., 2020; Arena et al., 2017; Gurajala et al., 2019). However, the phytoremediation method is a time-consuming process. To further improve the efficiency of phytoremediation, the combination of nanotechnology and phytoremediation for removing heavy metal pollutants has been proposed (Song et al., 2019; Deng et al., 2017).

Carbon nanomaterials, having large surface area and multiple functional groups on the surface or at the edge of the materials layers, possess exceptional immobilization effect on heavy metals by adsorption-precipitation. These phenomena may be related to electrostatic attraction and complex chemical reactions between heavy metal ions and the functional groups on carbon nanomaterials (Chen et al., 2018a, 2018b; Gao et al., 2008; RAO et al., 2007). Numerous studies have clearly demonstrated that carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have superior adsorption capacity and high adsorption efficiencies for various heavy metal ions, including lead, nickel, copper, chromium and cadmium ( Ihsanullah et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2008; Hu et al., 2009; Lu and Liu, 2006; Sadeghinya et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2008). Furthermore, it has been widely reported that various carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon dots and fullerene, can penetrate seed coats, stimulate germination, activate nutrient uptake, promote roots elongation and seedlings growth, and increase fruits and seeds yield in different plants (Avanasi et al., 2014; Khodakovskaya et al., 2009, 2012, 2013; Liu et al., 2013; Villagarcia et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2016). The combination of nano- and phyto-remediation is significantly more effective in degradation and removal of pollutants in contaminated soil and water when compared to either method applied independently (Torre-Roche et al., 2013; Hamdi, 2015; Song et al., 2019; Deng et al., 2017). It was demonstrated that CNTs could deliver different cargoes into different plant cell organelles (Liu et al., 2009). The interactive effects of Cd2+ and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) on Cd accumulation and Spartina alterniflora growth were also reported (Chai et al., 2013), which reveals that CNTs could mitigate Cd ions toxicity in plants and improve total Cd accumulation. In our previous research, the nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) can improve antioxidant capacity and reduce the reactive oxygen species content in Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings under heavy metal stress (Chen et al., 2020). Activity of the peroxidases (POD) extracted from N-CDs-treated seedlings was approximately 2.4 times and 2.8 times higher than control at different time points. Because of the promotion effect of the carbon nanomaterials on ion transport and antioxidant reaction, the carbon nanomaterials may have the ability to simultaneously promote Cd2+ adsorption and alleviate Cd2+ toxicity. However, the dosage of carbon nanomaterials has been consistently higher than 20 mg kg-1 or even higher than 1000 mg kg−1 in previous studies, causing concern for its own potential toxicity (Verna et al., 2019).

In this study, N-CDs with rich oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups are applied as the promotor and protector of plants under cadmium stress. N-CDs are amphoteric materials decorated with large number of carboxyl groups and amino groups, as well as ammonium, hydroxyl groups, ester groups, and carboxide to a lesser extent. We hypothesize that N-CDs are biological friendly and a low dose of N-CDs (4 mg kg−1) can produce significant protective effect on plants under Cd stress, such as the seeds germination, biomass, root development, pigments, and bioaccumulation of Cd. We tested these hypotheses with the model plant A. thaliana as well as with Cd-polluted water.

The graphite plates were provided by Beijing Xinna international Hi-Tech Material Co., Ltd., Beijing, China, purity >99%. The Murashige & Skoog (MS) culture medium was prepared according to the report of Murashige & Skoog in 1962 (Murashige and Skoog, 1962). All the other chemical reagents were analytical grade and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. The Arabidopsis thaliana（Columbia wild type）seeds were N-GMO and provided by Beijing Xinna international Hi-Tech Material Co., Ltd, Beijing, China.

N-CDs were synthesized in an electrolysis cell by using graphite plates as electrodes in 2.5% (Wt./Wt.) ammonia aqueous solution. When the solution became dark black, the sample solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant was freeze dried to yield N-CDs powders.

The microstructure of N-CDs materials was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-1011, Japan). The Raman spectrum was recorded by Raman spectrometer (invia, renishaw, Britain) to inspect imperfect graphene layer. The florescence spectrum was conducted on Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorimeter (Horiba, Japan) with slit-width of (3, 3). The examined solution was prepared by 1 mg kg
−1
and the pH is 6.8. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were performed in dried KBr pellets (about 1: 49 sample-to-KBr mass ratio) on Bruker Tensor 37 spectrometer at a range of 400–4000 cm−1 with 16 scans and a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded at 40eV pass energy with the Escalab 250Xi spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) irradiation source at 75.0 W.

The Cd2+ contents in aqueous solution and in plants were tested by an inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES, Optima 8000). Arabidopsis seedlings were cultured on 1/2 MS culture medium. The content of chlorophyll and polysaccharides were measured using ultraviolet–visible light spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, evolution 220).
The details of characterization methods are given in the methods section of supplementary materials.

The model organism A. thaliana was chosen to study the interaction between plants, cadmium and N-CDs. Arabidopsis (Columbia wild type) seeds were soaked in sterile DI water for 15 min, followed by sterilization in 5% (v/v) sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) aqueous solution for 5 min, then the sterilized seeds were thoroughly rinsed with sterile DI water for 3–5 times to remove the excessive NaClO. In the following experiments, seeds were cultivated on half-strength MS medium (1/2 MS medium) in artificial climate incubator in the conditions of 16h light/8h dark, light intensity 150 μmol m−2 s−1, day and night temperature 24/22 °C, and 50% humidity.

Adsorption of Cd
2+
in water. N-CDs materials were accurately weighed and added to 1000 ppm Cd2+ aqueous solution to a final N-CD concentration of 200 ppm. The mixture solution was ultrasound dispersed evenly and then stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The complexes were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min and Cd contents were measured in supernatant and sediment by ICP-AES. The sediment was then ultrasound dispersed in DI water to prepare samples for TEM observations.

Determination of Cd
2+ accumulation in plants. The sterilized Arabidopsis seeds were cultivated on 1/2 MS medium for 13 days, and then uniform seedlings were transplanted onto 1/2 MS medium with addition of a gradient of Cd2+ (10 mg kg −1 , 20 mg kg −1 , 50 mg kg −1 ) and N-CDs (0 mg kg −1, 4 mg kg −1 , 6 mg kg −1 , 8 mg kg −1 ) concentrations. In the final medium solution, the N-CDs powders were completely dispersed by sonication. For the premixed treatments, N-CDs were dispersed into Cd2+ solution by sonication for half an hour before adding into 1/2 MS culture medium. The N-CDs and Cd 2+ concentrations were consistent with the same treatments. The experiments were designed as Table S1. After 10 days, seedlings were harvested and rinsed carefully, blanched at 105 °C for 15min, and dried at 70 °C until reached a constant weight, the dry biomass was weighed up and recorded. The dry plants samples were milled and digested in HNO
3 •HClO
4
(4:1, v/v) for 8 h, and then placed on a hot plate until the solution became clear (Ryan et al., 2001). ICP-AES was used for determining the cadmium content (mg kg
−1
) in this cleared solution, and the cadmium content in each individual plant (mg/plant) was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs alleviate the toxicity of Cd2+ to plants
ce:section.ce:para: Seeds germination under Cd stress. 20 seeds with uniform size were selected and put in Petri dishes in which Cd-contaminated half-strength MS medium were added with or without the N-CDs. The Cd and N-CDs content in medium was 20 mg kg−1, and 4 mg kg−1, respectively. Treatment without N-CDs addition was included as the control group. Each experiment group was repeated three times, and the germination rate was recorded daily.

Effect of N-CDs on the growth and development of seedling under Cd stress. The tissue culture experiments were the same as the 2.2 section. After cultivating for 10 days, Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings were photographed and measured the fresh biomass, root lengths and dry biomass. The total chlorophyll content in desiccated leaves was extracted by ethanol and measured by spectrophotometry (Turner, 1981). The content of polysaccharides was determined in fresh leaves with phenol-sulfuric acid (Dubois et al., 1951). The malondialdehyde (MDA) content was determined in fresh leaves using thiobarbituric acid (Heath and Packer, 1968). The catalase (CAT) and peroxidase (POD) activities were measured by spectrophotometry (Gallego et al., 1996; Zhang et al., 1995). Details of the detection methods are given in methods section of supplementary materials.

Statistical Analysis. All of the collected statistics were presented as an average of 3 replicates. The data were statistically analyzed for Student's t-test or analysis of variance (ANOVA), and were presented as mean ± standard error of three replicates. All the statistical analyses were performed using Origin 8.0.

The amphoteric N-CDs with high surface area and multiple functional groups were synthesized through a one-step electrolytic method using graphite plates as the electrodes and ammonia aqueous solution as electrolyte. After lyophilization, the N-CDs powder is dark grey and very easily dispersed in water. The pH value of the N-CDs aqueous solutions of 1 mg ml−1 is about 6.8. Low magnification transmission electron microscope (TEM) images (Fig. 1 a) show that N-CDs are dots of 4–10 nm in size. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) observation from the edge of N-CDs show that the graphene (102) lattice fringe with spacing of 0.22 nm of each N-CDs nanodot can be clearly observed (Fig. 1b–c). The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows that the N-CDs crystal have multi defect because the relative intensity of the D peak and G peak is about 0.82. The fluorescence emission wavelength is about 550 nm when the excitation wavelength is 465 nm (Fig. 1e). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of lyophilized N-CDs (Fig. 1f) show that the main functional groups are –OH (3160 cm−1), –NH– (NH4 +, –NH2, 3408 cm−1), -COO- (1585 cm−1), OC-R (around 1700 cm−1) etc. The results of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are consisted with the FT-IR results. XPS spectra of the N-CDs are shown in Fig. 1g–i. The survey XPS spectrum indicates that N-CDs only contain C, O and N (Fig. 1g). High resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 1h) and N1s (Fig. 1i) reveal the bonding information of carbon (CO, CN, sp2 C) and nitrogen (-NH2, NH4 +, and CN) atoms. Therefore, N-CDs are carbon-based nanodots with high content of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. Because of the large quantity of carboxyl and amino groups, N-CDs are amphoteric and electronegative in aqueous solution with measured zeta potential of −15.68 mV.

Carbon nanomaterials decorated with organic groups have great potential as adsorbents for removing organic and inorganic pollutants including various divalent metal ions from aqueous solution (Hamdi, 2015; Xu et al., 2008). The N-CDs materials may have excellent adsorption capacity to heavy metal ions because of the large specific surface area and multiple electronegative functional groups. According to the adsorption curves, N-CDs show excellent adsorption capacity of Cd2+ in heavy metal polluted water, and its saturated adsorption weight is 559 mg g−1 (Fig. 2 b). From the TEM image inserted in Fig. 2b, the complex of N-CDs and Cd2+ (N-CDs-Cd) are much larger than that of N-CDs (Fig. 1b). While N-CDs are nanodots of 4–10 nm, the N-CDs-Cd particles are more than 1 μm. The major adsorption mechanism of metal ions by N-CDs might be the electrostatic attraction and the chemical reactions between the metal ions and the functional groups on the surface of the N-CDs materials (Hu et al., 2009; Li et al., 2003; Tofighy and Mohammadi, 2011). The large specific surface area provides ample adsorption sites for heavy metal ions by electrostatic attraction, and the rich functional groups at the edge of the materials further improved the adsorption capacity for heavy metal ions. The high content of carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino groups can increase the number of adsorption sites on materials, which can react with Cd2+ to form complexes in the solution and even precipitate ions from water. The supposed sorption mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 2a.

Previous reports have demonstrated that carbon nanoparticles could penetrate into plants cell wall and cell membrane and deliver different cargoes (such as water, ions and fertilizers, etc.) into plant organelles (He et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2009). Therefore, we hypothesize that N-CDs may also be able to deliver complexed cadmium into plants and raise the accumulation efficiency in plants used for phytoremediation. The effects of N-CDs on the Cd2+ bioaccumulation were performed on Arabidopsis (Columbia wild type). The effects of N-CDs on plants under heavy metal stress were compared with the control group in which seedlings were treated with no N-CDs. The N-CDs supplement led to significantly higher Cd2+ content in plant than the control groups (Fig. 3 ). The Cd contents of unit biomass (mg kg−1) in the N-CDs treated groups are 45.6%, 51.1%, 11.3% higher than the Cd contents in control under 10 mg kg−1, 20 mg kg−1 and 50 mg kg−1 Cd2+ stress, respectively. By calculating the total amount of Cd2+ accumulated by A. thaliana seedlings on different treated conditions, N-CDs increase Cd accumulation of individual plant (mg/plant) by about 97.9%, 113.3% and 88.8% on every seedling compared to the control group under 10 mg kg−1, 20 mg kg−1 and 50 mg kg−1 Cd2+ stress, respectively.

Carbon nanotubes have been shown to enhance plant accumulation of pollutants at low concentration and modulate the toxicity of heavy metal or pesticides in plants (Chai et al., 2013; Fan et al., 2018; Deng et al., 2017). It is interesting to note that the uptake of Cd ion by plants and plants tolerance to Cd are both enhanced in the presence of N-CDs even under extremely high Cd stress. On one hand, it is confirmed that the NH4 + can promote the Cd uptake and accumulation in the plant by regulating the rhizosphere condition (Cheng et al., 2016; Lakshmanan et al., 2012). The NH4 + can be exchanged by H+ after absorbed by roots and decrease the rhizosphere pH, then significantly more Cd2+ are available to be caught by plants. On the other hand, the N-CDs have super adsorption capacity for Cd ions. Consistent with previous reports, N-CDs, like other carbon-based nanomaterials, could be detected in Arabidopsis leaves and roots with both confocal microscopy and spectrofluorimetry based on its auto-fluorescence (Chen et al., 2020). Therefore, the N-CDs may also have high transport capacity to deliver metal ions into root and other organs of the plant. To further investigate the role of N-CDs in plant bioaccumulation of Cd, pretreatments were conducted by mixing Cd2+ ions and N-CDs prior to application to Arabidopsis seedlings. For the pretreated groups, N-CDs were sonicated to homogeneously dispersed in the Cd2+ solution before adding into medium. However, there is no positive effect on Cd2+ uptake in the premixed groups (Fig. 3a). To explain this observation, we speculate that at low and medium Cd2+ concentrations, the Cd2+ are immobilized by the N-CDs to become N-CDs-Cd complex. While the smaller complexes are easier to be transported into plants by the N-CDs, the larger ones are precipitated and cannot be uptaken by root. As a result, the Cd content in the seedlings in the pretreated medium is higher than the control but lower than that in the un-pretreated medium. At a high Cd concentration, the Cd content in the seedlings in the premixed medium is the lowest among the three groups. This result may be ascribed to the fact that the N-CDs-Cd complexes in pretreated medium are too large to be uptake by the cell membrane of the plant roots.

Above results reveal that N-CDs could not only accumulate Cd2+ in vitro, but also increase the Cd content in the plants and accumulate Cd2+ in vivo, indicating that N-CDs can act as a potential promoter to enhance the hyperaccumulator to uptake more heavy metals and then raise the phytoremediation efficiency. In addition to increasing Cd accumulation in Arabidopsis seedlings, N-CDs also appeared to improve plant tolerance against Cd2+ toxicity in our experiments under laboratory settings. Therefore, further studies were conducted to investigate the protective role of N-CDs in mitigating the growth retardation of A. thaliana seedlings caused by Cd2+.

Cadmium stress can lead to a significant negative effect on plants growth by inducing various phytotoxic symptoms, including germination and growth retardation, inhibition of photosynthesis, decrease in chlorophyll content and polysaccharide content (Aibibu et al., 2010; Chai et al., 2013; Li et al., 2003). The effect of N-CDs material on moderating the Cd toxicity were tested by measuring these physiological indices on Cd-exposed A. thaliana seedlings with or without N-CD supplementation.

N-CDs effectively accelerate Arabidopsis seed germination under different levels of Cd stress (Fig. 4b and S1). Fig. 3a show the germination rate when the Cd concentration is 20 mg kg−1. In the first two days, higher germination rates of 10.7% was demonstrated in N-CDs treated groups while none of the seeds in control geminated. In the following days, N-CDs further improved the germination rate by about 29.3% compared to the control when cultivated for 3 days. The almost equal final germination rates in each group were counted at the end of the 4-day cultivation. From Fig. S1, N-CDs also increased the germination rate under low and high Cd concentration. The germination rate in Cd10N4 is 2.5 times higher than Cd10 on the second day, and that in Cd50N4 is 2.2 times higher than Cd40 on the third day. Based on the results, N-CDs can act as a protector to generate positive effects on plants seeds germination process when the seeds are exposed to Cd.

On Cd-exposed seedlings, N-CDs addition significantly alleviate the toxicity caused by Cd stress (from 10 mg kg−1 to 50 mg kg−1), reducing total biomass loss, roots growth retardation and leaf chlorosis caused by Cd2+ (Fig. 4 b–g). The Cd-poisoned seedlings exposed to N-CDs over 10 days are visually greener and vigorous from phenotype (Fig. 4b), and have significantly higher fresh biomass (18.5–26.8%), dry biomass (25.0%–52.4%) and main root length (17.7%–35.9%) than the control. These growth benefits are likely the result of both reduced toxicity caused by Cd-immobilization in planta and direct growth-stimulating effect by N-CDs on plants similar to what has been observed with other carbon-based nano-materials (Wang et al., 2018; He et al., 2018).

The effects N-CDs-Cd complexes on plant physiology were also investigated using premixed materials (Fig. 4). Interestingly, the biomass of A. thaliana seedlings in premixed group decreases dramatically under middle and high Cd2+ stress (20 mg kg−1 and 50 mg kg−1) when compared with un-premixed group. We speculate that the adsorption of Cd ions and micronutrients in the growth media by N-CDs is competitive. When premixing the N-CDs with Cd ions in aqueous solution, more N-CDs are associated with Cd2+, and fewer N-CDs could carry nutrient into plants. Under low Cd stress, the toxic effect of Cd2+ on plants is greatly moderated by N-CDs and there are sufficient N-CDs can deliver more nutrient into the seedlings roots. As the Cd2+ concentration increases, more N-CDs are occupied by Cd2+ to form the N-CDs-Cd complexes. The smaller N-CDs-Cd complex in the medium of middle Cd concentration can be easier to be delivered into plants through root compared with control, which lead to the toxic effect of heavy metal enhanced. Meanwhile, with the decreased amount of N-CDs around the rhizosphere and on the surface of roots, the promotion effect of the N-CDs on plants growth and nutrient uptake decline. When the complex of N-CDs-Cd becomes too larger to be absorbed by root cell at the high Cd concentration of 50 mg kg−1, the toxicity of the Cd2+ to the plant is relieved. As a result, the biomass of the seedlings increases in the Cd50N4-p compared with that in the Cd50 (Fig. 4d–e). Although the biomass of each group are significantly different, the main root length changes slightly between pretreated group and un-pretreated group, implying that traces of N-CDs may have significant influence on plants roots.

Under environmental stresses, chlorophyll content is a major limiting factor in plant growth (Oloumi et al., 2018). Cd2+ uptake and accumulation can cause chlorophyll reduction in Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings (Abbas et al., 2017). Our results show that the N-CDs supplements could mitigate chlorophyll reduction caused by heavy metals (Cd 10–50 mg kg−1) (Fig. 4f). The total chlorophyll content in Arabidopsis leaves treated with N-CDs is significantly higher than no N-CD control at different Cd concentrations tested (Fig. 4f). Addition of N-CDs even doubles the photosynthetic pigment content in Arabidopsis thaliana leaves under high Cd2+ stress (Cd2+ 50 mg kg−1). The total chlorophyll content in Cd10N4, Cd20N4 and Cd50N4 is 1.52, 1. 67 and 2.03 times as high as that in Cd10, Cd20 and Cd50 group, respectively. In this experiment, we found little difference between the chlorophyll content of premixed groups and un-premixed groups at low and medium cadmium stress (Cd2+ 10 mg kg−1 and 20 mg kg−1). However, the total chlorophyll content in Cd50N4-p significantly increase by 54.3% than that in Cd50N4. The seedlings in Cd50N4-p have total chlorophyll of about 3.1 times as high as that in Cd50. The reason is speculated that the complex of N-CDs-Cd might have less toxic effect on the synthesis of photosynthetic pigments comparing with Cd2+. Polysaccharides are the product of photosynthesis in plants. N-CDs have significant positive effects on polysaccharides content in A. thaliana leaves (Fig. 4g). Under 20 mg kg−1 Cd stress, polysaccharides content in Cd20N4 is 6.0 times as high as that in the control. Even more, the polysaccharides content in N-CDs treated leaves is 8.67 times as high as that in the control under high Cd stress of 50 mg kg−1. It was reported that the growth inhibition caused by heavy metals is a consequence of metabolic disorders such as plasma membrane plasticity decreases owing to the heavy metal interactions with cell wall polysaccharides (Andrade et al., 2010; Seregin and Ivanov, 2001). This suggests that alleviation of Cd toxicity caused by N-CDs might be partly related with the increase of polysaccharides.

Plants can tolerate heavy metal toxicity through its innate antioxidation mechanisms [ref]. We have previously demonstrated that N-CDs could suppress reactive oxygen species (ROS) content and enhance peroxide oxidase activities in salt-stressed leaf tissues (Chen et al., 2020). Hence, we hypothesize that the enhanced Cd-tolerant phenotype observed in N-CDs-treated plants can be in part due to elevated antioxidant activities. The significantly decreased MDA content in N-CDs treated seedlings reveals that the N-CDs can indeed improve the ability of Cd-tolerance by reducing the oxidative damage in cells (Fig. 5 ). Consistently, the activity of catalase (CAT) and peroxidase (POD) in N-CDs treated leaves increased significantly by 173% and 45% than control, respectively.

The above results reveal that N-CDs had a unique ability of restoring biomass decrease, photosynthetic activity decline and oxidative damage caused by Cd toxicity. The growth retardation of Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings caused by Cd2+ is greatly mitigated by N-CDs supplement even in the extremely high Cd2+ content in vivo, indicating that N-CDs could act as a protector of plant in heavy metal contaminated areas. The protector means that the N-CDs addition can significantly alleviate the toxicity caused by high Cd stress on seedlings growth and development. As a result, N-CDs are effective candidates to mitigate heavy metal damage on seedlings growth and development under high Cd stress in vitro conditions.

To study the influence of the dosage of the N-CDs in culture medium, various supplemental dosage of N-CDs were added in culture medium. The Cd accumulation, biomass, chlorophyll content and polysaccharides content all respond positively to the increasing dosage of N-CDs. Higher N-CDs concentration in culture medium can cause higher Cd uptake, meanwhile increase biomass and chlorophyll content in seedlings compared to the control groups (Fig. S2). The Cd content of unit mass of seedlings in Cd20N8 is 58.3% higher than the control, as well as 12.6% higher than in Cd20N4. The Cd content in every individual seedling of Cd20N8 is 3.6 times as high as that in the control. Meanwhile, the biomass in Cd20N8 is 46.7% higher than that in Cd20N4, and the chlorophyll contents is also increased by 175.0%. The results reveal that higher dosage of N-CDs has higher positive impact on mitigating growth inhibition of plants under Cd stress by increasing plants biomass accumulation and chlorophyll content in leaves. With high content of N-CDs, more N-CDs can be used to absorb more Cd ions from medium in vitro and then transport Cd into plants for bioaccumulation.

In conclusion, we demonstrate that N-CDs materials are effective in enhancing Cd bioaccumulation and mitigating plants growth retardation of Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings under different levels of Cd stress. N-CDs present good biocompatibility and heavy metal adsorption capability in plants. The rich functional groups and nanoscale size of N-CDs could be important factors causing the physiological response of plants exposure to heavy metal ions with improved seed germination, biomass accumulation, root growth, total chlorophyll content, polysaccharide content and antioxidative enzyme activity. The potential application of N-CDs on hyperaccumulator can not only adsorb and immobilize Cd2+, preventing its leaching into the environment and increasing plant uptake efficiency, but also alleviate the damage caused by Cd2+ on plants, thereby enhancing the total capacity of phytoremediation.

Qiong Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft Beibei Liu: Validation, Investigation Han Man: Investigation Long Chen: Formal analysis, Data Curation Xiuli Wang: Resources, Methodology Jiangping Tu: Resources, Writing - Review & Editing Zhijiang Guo: Visualization, Data Curation, Project administration Gong Jin: Resources, Project administration Jun Lou: Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition Lijie Ci: Validation, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Startup Funding of Distinguished Professorship of “1000 Talents Program” (31370086963030), the high-level Talents' Discipline Construction Fund of Shandong University (31370089963078), the “Taishan Scholar Program” (11370085961006), Shandong Provincial Science and Technology Major Project (2016GGX104001,2017CXGC1010, 2018JMRH0211, ZR2017MEM002), and the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (2016JC005, 2017JC042 and 2017JC010) at Shandong University.
